message.content:
You are writing research documents about common flaws AI models/agents make when writing Rust. Write TWO files based on the comprehensive research data below.

## FILE 1: /workspace/test-workflows/research/rust-deep-dive.md

Write a comprehensive deep-dive document. Structure:

1. **Title and Executive Summary** — key stats (40%+ of compilation errors are ownership/borrowing, 60%+ faults are semantic, 19.7% hallucinated packages, ~29% baseline pass rate, 58.5% of LLMs can't generate immediately usable Rust, 1.7x more issues in AI PRs)
2. **Table of Contents**
3. **Why Rust is Particularly Challenging for AI** section (training data scarcity, unique ownership model, compiler strictness, non-local reasoning, different idiomatic patterns)
4. **12 Category Sections**, each with sub-flaws containing:
   - Description
   - Bad code example (```rust block)
   - Good code example (```rust block)
   - Why AI makes this mistake
   - Impact
   - Detection and mitigation
5. **Recommended Detection Toolchain** table
6. **Sources** section

Categories and flaws:

**1. Ownership and Borrowing Mistakes:**
- Returning references to locally-owned data — `fn get_first_word(s: String) -> &str` vs taking `&str`
- Simultaneous mutable and immutable borrows — read-then-modify patterns. Fix: copy value or restructure.
- Missing `move` in thread closures — `thread::spawn(|| ...)` vs `thread::spawn(move || ...)`

**2. Lifetime Annotation Errors:**
- Incorrect/unnecessary lifetime annotations — giving everything `'a` when lifetimes should differ
- Overusing borrowed references when owned types suffice — `struct Config<'a> { name: &'a str }` vs `struct Config { name: String }`

**3. Unsafe Code Misuse:**
- Unnecessary `unsafe` blocks to bypass borrow checker — `unsafe { *data.as_ptr().add(index) }` vs `data.get(index).copied()`
- deepSURF found 26 memory safety bugs including 6 unknown ones in unsafe Rust

**4. Error Handling (Unwrap Abuse):**
- Pervasive `.unwrap()` instead of `?` operator — `fs::read_to_string(path).unwrap()` vs `fs::read_to_string(path)?`
- Missing custom error types — should use `thiserror` for library code

**5. Trait Implementation Issues:**
- Object safety violations — `fn process(&self) -> Self` in trait used as `dyn Trait`
- `format!()` instead of `write!()` in Display impls — unnecessary allocation

**6. Concurrency Problems:**
- Holding locks across await points — must fetch data before locking
- Lock ordering inversions — need consistent ordering by address/ID
- Using async Mutex when sync Mutex suffices

**7. Performance Anti-Patterns (5 flaws):**
- Clone abuse to satisfy borrow checker — 3-5x slower than GC languages for naive clones. 10K vec: 622ns clone vs 41ns Arc
- `String` params instead of `&str`
- `&Vec<T>` instead of `&[T]`
- Unnecessary allocations in loops — move allocation outside, use `.clear()`
- Premature `.collect()` breaking iterator laziness

**8. Macro Misuse:**
- Wrong proc macro structure — confusing `proc_macro` builtin with external crate, using `format!()` instead of `quote!`

**9. Cargo/Dependency Issues:**
- Hallucinated crates — 19.7% of suggestions don't exist on crates.io, 43% repeat across queries
- Outdated crate versions/APIs — e.g., blocking `reqwest::get` vs async version

**10. Semantically Wrong Code:**
- Plausible but incorrect logic — SQLite reimpl example: 20,171x slowdown from missed rowid optimization, 1,857x from per-insert fsync
- Off-by-one and boundary errors — `slice[0]` without empty check, wrong range bounds

**11. Async Rust Mistakes:**
- Task cancellation — cleanup logic after await points may never execute. Use `scopeguard`.
- `select!` in loops recreating futures — losing buffered data. Pin futures outside loop.
- Future progress starvation — `handle_connection(stream).await` in accept loop instead of `tokio::spawn`
- Holding RAII guards across await points — connection pool exhaustion

**12. General AI Code Quality:**
- Excessive comments (90-100% occurrence in AI code)
- Refactoring avoidance — appending code instead of restructuring

Detection toolchain: `cargo check`, `cargo clippy -D warnings`, `clippy::unwrap_used`, `cargo fmt`, `cargo-geiger`, `cargo audit`, `cargo bench` + flamegraph, `proptest`, `cargo miri test`.

Include 28 source URLs.

## FILE 2: /workspace/test-workflows/research/rust-one-pager.md

Write a concise executive summary (~60-80 lines). Structure:
- Title
- Key statistics callout (3-5 stats in a blockquote)
- Top 5 most critical flaws — one paragraph + one short code snippet each
- Quick-reference detection checklist
- Link to deep-dive: `[Full Deep-Dive →](./rust-deep-dive.md)`

Write BOTH files now using the Write tool.

content.text: I'll create both files now. Let me first check the target directory exists.

input.content:
# AI-Generated Rust Code: Executive Summary of Common Flaws

> **Key Statistics:**
> - **40%+** of AI compilation errors stem from ownership and borrowing violations
> - **19.7%** of AI-suggested crate dependencies are hallucinated (do not exist on crates.io)
> - **58.5%** of LLMs cannot generate immediately usable Rust code
> - **~29%** baseline pass rate for AI-generated Rust on benchmarks
> - **1.7x** more issues found in AI-authored pull requests vs human-authored

---

## Top 5 Most Critical Flaws

### 1. Ownership and Borrowing Violations

Over 40% of compilation failures come from the AI misunderstanding Rust's ownership model. The most common pattern is taking ownership of data (e.g., `String` by value) and then trying to return a borrowed reference to it. The AI transfers assumptions from garbage-collected languages where returning views into parameters is always safe. These errors are caught by the compiler but require manual restructuring to fix.

```rust
// BAD: returns a reference to data that will be dropped
fn get_name(s: String) -> &str { &s[..] }
// GOOD: borrow the input instead
fn get_name(s: &str) -> &str { s }
```

### 2. Pervasive `.unwrap()` Instead of Error Handling

AI-generated Rust is riddled with `.unwrap()` calls on `Result` and `Option` types, converting every recoverable error into a panic. This comes from training on tutorial code where `.unwrap()` is used for brevity. In production, every `.unwrap()` is a crash site with an unhelpful error message.

```rust
// BAD: panics on any failure
let data = fs::read_to_string(path).unwrap();
// GOOD: propagate the error
let data = fs::read_to_string(path)?;
```

### 3. Hallucinated Dependencies

19.7% of crate names suggested by LLMs do not exist on crates.io, and 43% of these hallucinations repeat across queries. This creates a supply-chain attack vector: an attacker can register the hallucinated name and distribute malicious code. Common patterns include pluralization errors (`tokio-utils` instead of `tokio-util`) and plausible but nonexistent names.

```toml
# BAD: hallucinated crate name
tokio-utils = "0.7"
# GOOD: verified on crates.io
tokio-util = "0.7"
```

### 4. Semantically Correct but Catastrophically Slow Code

The most insidious flaw: code that compiles, passes tests, and looks correct but has extreme performance bugs. Research on AI-generated database implementations found a 20,171x slowdown from missing a rowid optimization and a 1,857x slowdown from per-insert `fsync`. The AI produces the right shape of code without understanding the performance characteristics of the underlying operations.

```rust
// BAD: fsync on every insert (1,857x slowdown)
fn insert(&mut self, row: Row) -> Result<()> {
    self.write(row)?;
    self.file.sync_all()?;  // should be batched per transaction
    Ok(())
}
```

### 5. Clone Abuse to Satisfy the Borrow Checker

When the AI cannot resolve a borrow checker error, it inserts `.clone()` to create owned copies. While this compiles, naive cloning can make Rust code 3-5x slower than garbage-collected languages. A 10,000-element `Vec<String>` takes ~622ns to clone vs ~41ns for an `Arc` reference count increment.

```rust
// BAD: cloning to avoid borrow issues
items.iter().map(|s| s.clone()).collect()
// GOOD: work with references or use Arc for shared ownership
items.iter().map(|s| s.to_uppercase()).collect()
```

---

## Quick-Reference Detection Checklist

- [ ] `cargo check` -- catches ownership, borrowing, and lifetime errors
- [ ] `cargo clippy -D warnings` -- catches idiomatic issues, unwrap abuse, performance anti-patterns
- [ ] `clippy::unwrap_used` (deny lint) -- flags all `.unwrap()` calls
- [ ] `cargo fmt` -- enforces consistent formatting
- [ ] `cargo-geiger` -- audits all `unsafe` usage
- [ ] `cargo audit` -- checks dependencies for known vulnerabilities
- [ ] Verify all crate names on [crates.io](https://crates.io) before adding
- [ ] `cargo bench` + flamegraph -- profiles performance-critical paths
- [ ] `proptest` -- property-based testing for boundary and edge cases
- [ ] `cargo miri test` -- detects undefined behavior in unsafe code

---

[Full Deep-Dive →](./rust-deep-dive.md)

content.text:
Both files have been written:

- `/workspace/test-workflows/research/rust-deep-dive.md` -- Comprehensive deep-dive covering all 12 categories with 20+ sub-flaws, each with bad/good code examples, root cause explanations, impact assessments, and detection strategies. Includes the executive summary with all key stats, a table of contents, the detection toolchain table, and 28 source references.

- `/workspace/test-workflows/research/rust-one-pager.md` -- Concise executive summary (~75 lines) with the 5 key statistics in a blockquote, the top 5 most critical flaws (each with a paragraph and code snippet), a quick-reference detection checklist, and a link to the deep-dive.
